ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for plain cracker; SNOMED CT coverage of generic snack crackers is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of plain crackers is approximately 4 crackers (about 20 g), commonly eaten as a snack or paired with spreads, cheese, or dips.

nutrient_source.title: Plain crackers — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Foodo record for plain crackers (cr_id F11080045). Sodium is recorded as -1 in the HPB source, indicating the value was not reported. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for crackers, standard (NDB 18228) as the closest approved secondary source. Calcium and iron reported as 0.0 in HPB source; confirmed as trace/negligible per USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 74 (±4) is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain water crackers / soda crackers (wheat flour base, no added sugar). This is the closest approved analogous entry for plain baked wheat crackers. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 74 × 8.23 / 100 ≈ 6.1 (serving size 20 g, available carbohydrate 41.13 g/100 g × 0.20 = 8.23 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content not reported (recorded as -1 in HPB source); estimated sodium for commercial plain crackers is approximately 300–500 mg/100 g based on USDA SR Legacy data for comparable products
hypertension.guidance: Sodium data is unavailable in the primary source; individuals with hypertension should check product packaging for actual sodium content and choose low-sodium variants (<200 mg/100 g) or limit portion size to reduce cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~74) combined with 41.1 g/100 g refined carbohydrate and zero dietary fibre produces rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain crackers have a high GI (~74) and zero dietary fibre; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 2–3 crackers, pair with protein or healthy fat to blunt glycaemic response, and prefer wholegrain or high-fibre cracker alternatives (dietary fibre >3 g/100 g).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) from vegetable oil processing and uncertain sodium content; low saturated fat (1.5 g/100 g) and zero cholesterol are partially favourable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain crackers may contain trace trans fat from partially hydrogenated vegetable oils; individuals with cardiovascular disease should verify ingredient labels for hydrogenated oils, monitor sodium intake, and choose wholegrain alternatives with added seeds to improve the lipid and fibre profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plain crackers are simple baked snack products made primarily from refined wheat flour, water, vegetable oil, and a small amount of salt. They are produced by mixing the dough, rolling it thin, and baking at high temperature until crisp and dry. The texture is hard and brittle with a neutral, mildly savoury flavour and no added sweeteners or flavourings. Plain crackers are a globally ubiquitous snack food consumed across many cultures as a standalone snack, a vehicle for spreads (butter, peanut butter, hummus), or an accompaniment to soups and salads. Nutritionally, plain crackers are a moderate-energy, low-fat, high-carbohydrate food providing approximately 246 kcal per 100 g, with 41.1 g refined carbohydrate, 5.9 g protein, and 6.4 g fat. Dietary fibre is negligible (0 g/100 g), and sugar is absent. Sodium data is not available in the primary HPB source and must be verified from product packaging; commercial variants typically contain 300–500 mg sodium per 100 g. The high glycemic index (~74) and absence of dietary fibre make plain crackers unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome without careful portion control. The uncertain sodium content raises concern for individuals with hypertension. Trace trans fat from vegetable oil processing is a minor cardiovascular concern. Wholegrain or seed-enriched cracker alternatives are clinically preferable for most chronic disease management contexts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in plain crackers?
What is the GI of plain crackers?
Are plain crackers suitable for hypertension?
Are water crackers the same as plain crackers?
Can I eat crackers on a low-carb diet?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo canonical record (cr_id F11080045, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and fatty acid profile estimated from USDA SR Legacy as the closest approved secondary source. Sodium is genuinely unavailable in the HPB record (-1 sentinel). GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for plain wheat crackers.
notes: sodium_mg is set to -1: the HPB Foodo source for this record (cr_id F11080045) did not report a sodium value; the field is recorded as -1 per the sentinel convention. Estimated sodium for commercial plain crackers is approximately 300–500 mg/100 g based on USDA SR Legacy data for comparable products (NDB 18228), but this estimate has not been substituted into the per_100g field to preserve source fidelity. Users should verify sodium from product packaging. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1 where detailed micronutrient data is not available in the HPB Foodo source for this record; USDA SR Legacy provides partial fatty acid data used for saturated_fat_g estimation only. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — plain crackers contain no added sugar and negligible intrinsic sugar by formulation (confirmed zero). dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — refined wheat flour crackers without added fibre contain negligible dietary fibre (confirmed zero). calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; these are treated as confirmed trace/negligible per USDA SR Legacy cross-reference. The dietary_tags field in the input contained 'low_sodium' which is inconsistent with the -1 sodium sentinel and estimated sodium of 300–500 mg/100 g; this tag has been removed from the output as unsupported. Trans fat estimated at ~0.1 g/100 g based on USDA SR Legacy data for comparable plain crackers; this is a trace amount from vegetable oil processing.

Added zero-macro justification in notes for sugar_g and dietary_fibre_g (confirmed zero per HPB).
